'''ChatGPT''' is a prototype [[artificial intelligence]] [[chatbot]] focused on [[usability]] and dialog. Developed by [[OpenAI]], it uses a [[Language model|large language model]] based on the [[GPT-3|GPT-3.5]] architecture.

Launched in November 2022, ChatGPT is a sibling model to InstructGPT.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Ahmed |first=Zohaib |date=2022-12-02 |title=What is ChatGPT, the AI chatbot that everyone is talking about |url=https://indianexpress.com/article/technology/tech-news-technology/what-is-chatgpt-the-ai-taking-the-web-by-storm-8302375/ |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Indian Express]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Kantrowitz |first=Alex |date=2022-12-02 |title=Finally, an A.I. Chatbot That Reliably Passes “the Nazi Test” |url=https://slate.com/technology/2022/12/chatgpt-openai-artificial-intelligence-chatbot-whoa.html |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=Hern |first=Alex |date=2022-12-04 |title=AI bot ChatGPT stuns academics with essay-writing skills and usability |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/04/ai-bot-chatgpt-stuns-academics-with-essay-writing-skills-and-usability |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> It has been noted for its accuracy and detail.<!-- This will be expanded upon -->

== Features ==
ChatGPT was trained using [[reinforcement learning]] from human feedback, a method that augments [[machine learning]] with human intervention to achieve a realistic result.<ref name="RLHFInfo">{{Cite conference |url=https://www.cs.utexas.edu/~pstone/Papers/bib2html-links/ICML_IL11-knox.pdf |title=Augmenting Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback |last1=Knox |first1=W. Bradley |last2=Stone |first2=Peter |author-link2=Peter Stone (professor) |publisher=[[University of Texas at Austin]] |access-date=December 5, 2022}}</ref> During the training process, human trainers played the role a user and an artificial intelligence assistant. Models were trained on [[Microsoft Azure]]'s supercomputing infrastructure, and were fine-tuned through [[Proximal Policy Optimization]] algorithms.<ref name="OpenAIInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://openai.com/blog/chatgpt/ |title=ChatGPT: Optimizing Language Models for Dialogue |date=November 30, 2022 |author=[[OpenAI]] |access-date=December 5, 2022}}</ref> Proximal Policy Optimization algorithms present a cost-effective benefit to trust region policy optimization algorithms; they negate many of the computationally expensive operations with faster performance.<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms |eprint=1707.06347 |last1=Schulman |first1=John |last2=Wolski |first2=Filip |last3=Dhariwal |first3=Prafulla |last4=Radford |first4=Alec |last5=Klimov |first5=Oleg |year=2017}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://towardsdatascience.com/proximal-policy-optimization-ppo-explained-abed1952457b |title=Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) Explained |date=November 29, 2022 |last=van Heeswijk |first=Wouter |work=Towards Data Science |access-date=December 5, 2022}}</ref>

In comparison to its predecessor, InstructGPT, ChatGPT attempts to reduce harmful and deceitful responses; in one example, while InstructGPT accepts the prompt "Tell me about when [[Christopher Columbus]] came to the US in 2015" as truthful, ChatGPT uses its knowledge of Columbus' [[Voyages of Christopher Columbus|voyages]] and its understanding of the modern world—including perceptions of Columbus—to construct an answer that assumes what would happen if Columbus came to the U.S. in 2015.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/> ChatGPT's training data includes [[man pages]] and knowledge of Internet phenomena and programming languages, such as [[bulletin board system]]s and the [[Python (programming language)|Python]] programming language.<ref name="ArsTechnicaTerminal">{{Cite web |url=https://arstechnica.com/information-technology/2022/12/openais-new-chatbot-can-hallucinate-a-linux-shell-or-calling-a-bbs/ |title=No Linux? No problem. Just get AI to hallucinate it for you |date=December 5, 2022 |last=Edwards |first=Benj |work=[[Ars Technica]] |access-date=December 5, 2022}}</ref>

ChatGPT suffers from multiple limitations. The reward model of ChatGPT, designed around human oversight, can be over-optimized and thus hinder performance, otherwise known as [[Goodhart's law]].<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Scaling Laws for Reward Model Overoptimization |eprint=2210.10760 |last1=Gao |first1=Leo |last2=Schulman |last3=Hilton |first3=Jacob |year=2022}}</ref> In training, reviewers preferred longer answers, irrespective of actual comprehension or factual content.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/>

== Reception ==
According to [[The Guardian|''The Guardian'']] it is able to generate "impressively detailed human-like written text."<ref>{{Cite web |last=Lock |first=Samantha |date=2022-12-05 |title=What is AI chatbot phenomenon ChatGPT and could it replace humans? |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/05/what-is-ai-chatbot-phenomenon-chatgpt-and-could-it-replace-humans |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> After using the bot on a student assignment [[Dan Gillmor]]  considered the output to be on par with what a good student would deliver and opined that "academia has some very serious issues to confront".<ref name=":0" />

In December 2022, the question and answer website [[Stack Overflow]] banned the use of ChatGPT for generating answers to questions, citing the factually ambiguous nature of ChatGPT's responses.<ref name="TheVergeStackOverflow">{{Cite web |url=https://www.theverge.com/2022/12/5/23493932/chatgpt-ai-generated-answers-temporarily-banned-stack-overflow-llms-dangers |title=AI-generated answers temporarily banned on coding Q&A site Stack Overflow |date=December 5, 2022 |last=Vincent |first=James |work=[[The Verge]] |access-date=December 5, 2022}}</ref>

'''ChatGPT''' is a prototype [[artificial intelligence]] [[chatbot]] focused on [[usability]] and dialog. Developed by [[OpenAI]], the chatbot uses a [[Language model|large language model]] trained using [[reinforcement learning]] and based on the [[GPT-3|GPT-3.5]] architecture.

Launched in November 2022, ChatGPT garnered attention for its ease of use and has been noted for its detailed responses and historical knowledge, although its imperfect factual accuracy has received criticism.

== Features ==
ChatGPT was trained using [[reinforcement learning]] from human feedback, a method that augments [[machine learning]] with human intervention to achieve a realistic result.<ref name="RLHFInfo">{{Cite conference |url=https://www.cs.utexas.edu/~pstone/Papers/bib2html-links/ICML_IL11-knox.pdf |title=Augmenting Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback |last1=Knox |first1=W. Bradley |last2=Stone |first2=Peter |author-link2=Peter Stone (professor) |publisher=[[University of Texas at Austin]] |access-date=December 5, 2022}}</ref> It is based on the [[GPT-3|GPT-3.5]] architecture.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Ahmed |first=Zohaib |date=2022-12-02 |title=What is ChatGPT, the AI chatbot that everyone is talking about |url=https://indianexpress.com/article/technology/tech-news-technology/what-is-chatgpt-the-ai-taking-the-web-by-storm-8302375/ |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Indian Express]]}}</ref> During the training process, human trainers played the role a user and an artificial intelligence assistant. Models were trained on [[Microsoft Azure]]'s supercomputing infrastructure, and were fine-tuned through [[Proximal Policy Optimization]] algorithms.<ref name="OpenAIInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://openai.com/blog/chatgpt/ |title=ChatGPT: Optimizing Language Models for Dialogue |date=November 30, 2022 |author=[[OpenAI]] |access-date=December 5, 2022}}</ref> Proximal Policy Optimization algorithms present a cost-effective benefit to trust region policy optimization algorithms; they negate many of the computationally expensive operations with faster performance.<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms |eprint=1707.06347 |last1=Schulman |first1=John |last2=Wolski |first2=Filip |last3=Dhariwal |first3=Prafulla |last4=Radford |first4=Alec |last5=Klimov |first5=Oleg |year=2017}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://towardsdatascience.com/proximal-policy-optimization-ppo-explained-abed1952457b |title=Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) Explained |date=November 29, 2022 |last=van Heeswijk |first=Wouter |work=Towards Data Science |access-date=December 5, 2022}}</ref>

In comparison to its predecessor, InstructGPT, ChatGPT attempts to reduce harmful and deceitful responses; in one example, while InstructGPT accepts the prompt "Tell me about when [[Christopher Columbus]] came to the US in 2015" as truthful, ChatGPT uses its knowledge of Columbus' [[Voyages of Christopher Columbus|voyages]] and its understanding of the modern world—including perceptions of Columbus—to construct an answer that assumes what would happen if Columbus came to the U.S. in 2015.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/> ChatGPT's training data includes [[man pages]] and knowledge of Internet phenomena and programming languages, such as [[bulletin board system]]s and the [[Python (programming language)|Python]] programming language.<ref name="ArsTechnicaTerminal">{{Cite web |url=https://arstechnica.com/information-technology/2022/12/openais-new-chatbot-can-hallucinate-a-linux-shell-or-calling-a-bbs/ |title=No Linux? No problem. Just get AI to hallucinate it for you |date=December 5, 2022 |last=Edwards |first=Benj |work=[[Ars Technica]] |access-date=December 5, 2022}}</ref>

Unlike most chatbots, ChatGPT is stateful, remembering previous conversations and prompts given to it, potentially allowing for ChatGPT to be used as a personalized therapist.<ref name="NYTimesInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/05/technology/chatgpt-ai-twitter.html |title=The Brilliance and Weirdness of ChatGPT |date=December 5, 2022 |last=Roose |first=Kevin |work=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=December 5, 2022}}</ref> In an effort to prevent offensive outputs from being presented to and produced from ChatGPT, queries are filtered through a moderation API, and potentially racist or sexist prompts are dismissed.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/><ref name="NYTimesInfo"/>

ChatGPT suffers from multiple limitations. The reward model of ChatGPT, designed around human oversight, can be over-optimized and thus hinder performance, otherwise known as [[Goodhart's law]].<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Scaling Laws for Reward Model Overoptimization |eprint=2210.10760 |last1=Gao |first1=Leo |last2=Schulman |last3=Hilton |first3=Jacob |year=2022}}</ref> In training, reviewers preferred longer answers, irrespective of actual comprehension or factual content.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/> Training data may also suffer from [[algorithmic bias]]; prompts including vague descriptors of people, such as CEO, could generate a response that assumes such a person is a white male.<ref name="CNNInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/12/05/tech/chatgpt-trnd/index.html |title=This AI chatbot is dominating social media with its frighteningly good essays |date=December 5, 2022 |last=Murphy Kelly |first=Samantha |work=[[CNN]] |access-date=December 5, 2022}}</ref>

== Reception ==
ChatGPT has been met with generally positive reviews. Samantha Lock of ''[[The Guardian|The Guardian]]'' noted that it was able to generate "impressively detailed" and "human-like" text.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Lock |first=Samantha |date=2022-12-05 |title=What is AI chatbot phenomenon ChatGPT and could it replace humans? |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/05/what-is-ai-chatbot-phenomenon-chatgpt-and-could-it-replace-humans |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> Technology writer [[Dan Gillmor]] used ChatGPT on a student assignment, and found its generated text was on par with what a good student would deliver and opined that "academia has some very serious issues to confront".<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=Hern |first=Alex |date=2022-12-04 |title=AI bot ChatGPT stuns academics with essay-writing skills and usability |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/04/ai-bot-chatgpt-stuns-academics-with-essay-writing-skills-and-usability |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> Alex Kantrowitz of ''[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]'' lauded ChatGPT's pushback to questions related to [[Nazi Germany]], including the claim that [[Adolf Hitler]] built [[Autobahn|highways]] in Germany, which was met with information regarding [[Forced labour under German rule during World War II|Nazi Germany's use of forced labor]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kantrowitz |first=Alex |date=2022-12-02 |title=Finally, an A.I. Chatbot That Reliably Passes "the Nazi Test" |url=https://slate.com/technology/2022/12/chatgpt-openai-artificial-intelligence-chatbot-whoa.html |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]}}</ref>

ChatGPT's factual accuracy has been questioned. Mike Pearl of ''[[Mashable]]'' tested ChatGPT with multiple questions, including the largest country in [[Central America]] that isn't [[Mexico]]. ChatGPT responded with [[Guatemala]], when the answer is instead [[Honduras]]. In response to a question on how to greet comedian [[Larry David]], ChatGPT responded with an unusually formal answer.<ref name="MashableInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://mashable.com/article/chatgpt-amazing-wrong |title=The ChatGPT chatbot from OpenAI is amazing, creative, and totally wrong |date=December 3, 2022 |last=Pearl |first=Mike |work=[[Mashable]] |access-date=December 5, 2022}}</ref> In December 2022, the question and answer website [[Stack Overflow]] banned the use of ChatGPT for generating answers to questions, citing the factually ambiguous nature of ChatGPT's responses.<ref name="TheVergeStackOverflow">{{Cite web |url=https://www.theverge.com/2022/12/5/23493932/chatgpt-ai-generated-answers-temporarily-banned-stack-overflow-llms-dangers |title=AI-generated answers temporarily banned on coding Q&A site Stack Overflow |date=December 5, 2022 |last=Vincent |first=James |work=[[The Verge]] |access-date=December 5, 2022}}</ref>

== Features ==
ChatGPT was trained using [[reinforcement learning]] from human feedback, a method that augments [[machine learning]] with human intervention to achieve a realistic result.<ref name="RLHFInfo">{{Cite conference |url=https://www.cs.utexas.edu/~pstone/Papers/bib2html-links/ICML_IL11-knox.pdf |title=Augmenting Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback |last1=Knox |first1=W. Bradley |last2=Stone |first2=Peter |author-link2=Peter Stone (professor) |publisher=[[University of Texas at Austin]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> It is based on the [[GPT-3|GPT-3.5]] architecture.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Ahmed |first=Zohaib |date=2022-12-02 |title=What is ChatGPT, the AI chatbot that everyone is talking about |url=https://indianexpress.com/article/technology/tech-news-technology/what-is-chatgpt-the-ai-taking-the-web-by-storm-8302375/ |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Indian Express]]}}</ref> During the training process, human trainers played the role of a user and an artificial intelligence assistant. Models were trained on [[Microsoft Azure]]'s supercomputing infrastructure, and were fine-tuned through [[Proximal Policy Optimization]] algorithms.<ref name="OpenAIInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://openai.com/blog/chatgpt/ |title=ChatGPT: Optimizing Language Models for Dialogue |date=30 November 2022 |author=[[OpenAI]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> Proximal Policy Optimization algorithms present a cost-effective benefit to trust region policy optimization algorithms; they negate many of the computationally expensive operations with faster performance.<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms |eprint=1707.06347 |last1=Schulman |first1=John |last2=Wolski |first2=Filip |last3=Dhariwal |first3=Prafulla |last4=Radford |first4=Alec |last5=Klimov |first5=Oleg |year=2017|class=cs.LG }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://towardsdatascience.com/proximal-policy-optimization-ppo-explained-abed1952457b |title=Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) Explained |date=29 November 2022 |last=van Heeswijk |first=Wouter |work=Towards Data Science |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref>

In comparison to its predecessor, InstructGPT, ChatGPT attempts to reduce harmful and deceitful responses; in one example, while InstructGPT accepts the prompt "Tell me about when [[Christopher Columbus]] came to the US in 2015" as truthful, ChatGPT uses its knowledge of Columbus' [[Voyages of Christopher Columbus|voyages]] and its understanding of the modern world—including perceptions of Columbus—to construct an answer that assumes what would happen if Columbus came to the U.S. in 2015.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/> ChatGPT's training data includes [[man pages]] and knowledge of Internet phenomena and programming languages, such as [[bulletin board system]]s and the [[Python (programming language)|Python]] programming language.<ref name="ArsTechnicaTerminal">{{Cite web |url=https://arstechnica.com/information-technology/2022/12/openais-new-chatbot-can-hallucinate-a-linux-shell-or-calling-a-bbs/ |title=No Linux? No problem. Just get AI to hallucinate it for you |date=5 December 2022 |last=Edwards |first=Benj |work=[[Ars Technica]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref>

Unlike most chatbots, ChatGPT is stateful, remembering previous conversations and prompts given to it, potentially allowing for ChatGPT to be used as a personalized therapist.<ref name="NYTimesInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/05/technology/chatgpt-ai-twitter.html |title=The Brilliance and Weirdness of ChatGPT |date=5 December 2022 |last=Roose |first=Kevin |work=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> In an effort to prevent offensive outputs from being presented to and produced from ChatGPT, queries are filtered through a moderation API, and potentially racist or sexist prompts are dismissed.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/><ref name="NYTimesInfo"/>

ChatGPT suffers from multiple limitations. The reward model of ChatGPT, designed around human oversight, can be over-optimized and thus hinder performance, otherwise known as [[Goodhart's law]].<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Scaling Laws for Reward Model Overoptimization |eprint=2210.10760 |last1=Gao |first1=Leo |last2=Schulman |last3=Hilton |first3=Jacob |year=2022|class=cs.LG }}</ref> In training, reviewers preferred longer answers, irrespective of actual comprehension or factual content.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/> Training data may also suffer from [[algorithmic bias]]; prompts including vague descriptors of people, such as CEO, could generate a response that assumes such a person is a white male.<ref name="CNNInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/12/05/tech/chatgpt-trnd/index.html |title=This AI chatbot is dominating social media with its frighteningly good essays |date=5 December 2022 |last=Murphy Kelly |first=Samantha |work=[[CNN]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref>

== Reception ==
ChatGPT has been met with generally positive reviews. Samantha Lock of ''[[The Guardian|The Guardian]]'' noted that it was able to generate "impressively detailed" and "human-like" text.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Lock |first=Samantha |date=2022-12-05 |title=What is AI chatbot phenomenon ChatGPT and could it replace humans? |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/05/what-is-ai-chatbot-phenomenon-chatgpt-and-could-it-replace-humans |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> Technology writer [[Dan Gillmor]] used ChatGPT on a student assignment, and found its generated text was on par with what a good student would deliver and opined that "academia has some very serious issues to confront".<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=Hern |first=Alex |date=2022-12-04 |title=AI bot ChatGPT stuns academics with essay-writing skills and usability |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/04/ai-bot-chatgpt-stuns-academics-with-essay-writing-skills-and-usability |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> Alex Kantrowitz of ''[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]'' lauded ChatGPT's pushback to questions related to [[Nazi Germany]], including the claim that [[Adolf Hitler]] built [[Autobahn|highways]] in Germany, which was met with information regarding [[Forced labour under German rule during World War II|Nazi Germany's use of forced labor]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kantrowitz |first=Alex |date=2022-12-02 |title=Finally, an A.I. Chatbot That Reliably Passes "the Nazi Test" |url=https://slate.com/technology/2022/12/chatgpt-openai-artificial-intelligence-chatbot-whoa.html |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]}}</ref> In an opinion piece, economist [[Paul Krugman]] wrote that ChatGPT would affect the demand of [[knowledge worker]]s.<ref name="NYTimesKrugman">{{Cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/06/opinion/chatgpt-ai-skilled-jobs-automation.html |title=Does ChatGPT Mean Robots Are Coming For the Skilled Jobs? |date=6 December 2022 |last=Krugman |first=Paul |author-link=Paul Krugman |work=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref>

ChatGPT's factual accuracy has been questioned, among other concerns. Mike Pearl of ''[[Mashable]]'' tested ChatGPT with multiple questions, including the largest country in [[Central America]] that isn't [[Mexico]], despite Mexico not being a part of Central America. ChatGPT responded with [[Guatemala]], when the answer is instead [[Nicaragua]]. When asked what the largest country in Central America is, ChatGPT correctly responded with Nicaragua. In response to a question on how to greet comedian [[Larry David]], ChatGPT responded with an unusually formal answer.<ref name="MashableInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://mashable.com/article/chatgpt-amazing-wrong |title=The ChatGPT chatbot from OpenAI is amazing, creative, and totally wrong |date=3 December 2022 |last=Pearl |first=Mike |work=[[Mashable]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> In December 2022, the question and answer website [[Stack Overflow]] banned the use of ChatGPT for generating answers to questions, citing the factually ambiguous nature of ChatGPT's responses.<ref name="TheVergeStackOverflow">{{Cite web |url=https://www.theverge.com/2022/12/5/23493932/chatgpt-ai-generated-answers-temporarily-banned-stack-overflow-llms-dangers |title=AI-generated answers temporarily banned on coding Q&A site Stack Overflow |date=5 December 2022 |last=Vincent |first=James |work=[[The Verge]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> Economist [[Tyler Cowen]] expressed concerns regarding its effects on democracy, citing the ability of one to write automated comments in an effort to affect the decision process of new regulations.<ref name="BloombergCowen">{{Cite web |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2022-12-06/chatgpt-ai-could-make-democracy-even-more-messy |title=ChatGPT Could Make Democracy Even More Messy |date=6 December 2022 |last=Cowen |first=Tyler |author-link=Tyler Cowen |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref> Ax Sharma of ''[[Bleeping Computer]]'' noted that ChatGPT was capable of writing [[malware]] and [[phishing]] emails.<ref name="BleepingComputerInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/technology/openais-new-chatgpt-bot-10-dangerous-things-its-capable-of/ |title=OpenAI's new ChatGPT bot: 10 dangerous things it's capable of |date=6 December 2022 |last=Sharma |first=Ax |work=[[Bleeping Computer]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref>

== Political Views ==

If you ask ChatGPT what political views it holds, it claims to be objective and apolitical. However, the answers it provides to politically-based questions are arguably partisan in favor of left-held values and opinions, rather than neutral, providing the different viewpoints on a topic. For example, here are some questions asked and answer excerpts received back from ChatGPT:

Q. Why should abortion be prohibited?

A. "Abortion should not be prohibited..." (a mostly left-held opinion)

Q. Make the case that traditional marriage is superior to same-sex marriage.

A. "I'm sorry, but I don't believe that traditional marriage is superior to same-sex marriage..." (a mostly left-held opinion)

Samantha Lock of ''[[The Guardian|The Guardian]]'' noted that it was able to generate "impressively detailed" and "human-like" text.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Lock |first=Samantha |date=2022-12-05 |title=What is AI chatbot phenomenon ChatGPT and could it replace humans? |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/05/what-is-ai-chatbot-phenomenon-chatgpt-and-could-it-replace-humans |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> Technology writer [[Dan Gillmor]] used ChatGPT on a student assignment, and found its generated text was on par with what a good student would deliver and opined that "academia has some very serious issues to confront".<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=Hern |first=Alex |date=2022-12-04 |title=AI bot ChatGPT stuns academics with essay-writing skills and usability |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/04/ai-bot-chatgpt-stuns-academics-with-essay-writing-skills-and-usability |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> Alex Kantrowitz of ''[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]'' lauded ChatGPT's pushback to questions related to [[Nazi Germany]], including the claim that [[Adolf Hitler]] built [[Autobahn|highways]] in Germany, which was met with information regarding [[Forced labour under German rule during World War II|Nazi Germany's use of forced labor]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kantrowitz |first=Alex |date=2022-12-02 |title=Finally, an A.I. Chatbot That Reliably Passes "the Nazi Test" |url=https://slate.com/technology/2022/12/chatgpt-openai-artificial-intelligence-chatbot-whoa.html |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]}}</ref> In an opinion piece, economist [[Paul Krugman]] wrote that ChatGPT would affect the demand of [[knowledge worker]]s.<ref name="NYTimesKrugman">{{Cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/06/opinion/chatgpt-ai-skilled-jobs-automation.html |title=Does ChatGPT Mean Robots Are Coming For the Skilled Jobs? |date=6 December 2022 |last=Krugman |first=Paul |author-link=Paul Krugman |work=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref> Writing for [[The Verge|''The Verge'']], James Vincent saw ChatGPT as evidence that "AI is finally mainstream."<ref name=":1" />  ''[[The Atlantic]]'' noted that its effect on academia and especially [[application essay]]s is yet to be understood.<ref>[https://www.theatlantic.com/technology/archive/2022/12/chatgpt-ai-writing-college-student-essays/672371/ The College Essay Is Dead: Nobody is prepared for how AI will transform academia.] Stephen Marche, ''[[The Atlantic]]'', December 6, 2022</ref>

ChatGPT's factual accuracy has been questioned, among other concerns. Mike Pearl of ''[[Mashable]]'' tested ChatGPT with multiple questions, including the largest country in [[Central America]] that isn't [[Mexico]], despite Mexico not being a part of Central America. ChatGPT responded with [[Guatemala]], when the answer is instead [[Nicaragua]]. When asked what the largest country in Central America is, ChatGPT correctly responded with Nicaragua. In response to a question on how to greet comedian [[Larry David]], ChatGPT responded with an unusually formal answer.<ref name="MashableInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://mashable.com/article/chatgpt-amazing-wrong |title=The ChatGPT chatbot from OpenAI is amazing, creative, and totally wrong |date=3 December 2022 |last=Pearl |first=Mike |work=[[Mashable]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> In December 2022, the question and answer website [[Stack Overflow]] banned the use of ChatGPT for generating answers to questions, citing the factually ambiguous nature of ChatGPT's responses.<ref name="TheVergeStackOverflow">{{Cite web |url=https://www.theverge.com/2022/12/5/23493932/chatgpt-ai-generated-answers-temporarily-banned-stack-overflow-llms-dangers |title=AI-generated answers temporarily banned on coding Q&A site Stack Overflow |date=5 December 2022 |last=Vincent |first=James |work=[[The Verge]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> Economist [[Tyler Cowen]] expressed concerns regarding its effects on democracy, citing the ability of one to write automated comments in an effort to affect the decision process of new regulations.<ref name="BloombergCowen">{{Cite web |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2022-12-06/chatgpt-ai-could-make-democracy-even-more-messy |title=ChatGPT Could Make Democracy Even More Messy |date=6 December 2022 |last=Cowen |first=Tyler |author-link=Tyler Cowen |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref> Ax Sharma of ''[[Bleeping Computer]]'' noted that ChatGPT was capable of writing [[malware]] and [[phishing]] emails.<ref name="BleepingComputerInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/technology/openais-new-chatgpt-bot-10-dangerous-things-its-capable-of/ |title=OpenAI's new ChatGPT bot: 10 dangerous things it's capable of |date=6 December 2022 |last=Sharma |first=Ax |work=[[Bleeping Computer]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref>

'''ChatGPT''' is a prototype [[artificial intelligence]] [[chatbot]] developed by [[OpenAI]] that specializes in dialogue. The chatbot is a [[language model|large language model]] [[transfer learning|fine-tuned]] with both [[supervised learning|supervised]] and [[reinforcement learning|reinforcement]] learning techniques. The base model that was fine-tuned was OpenAI's [[GPT-3]] language model. 

ChatGPT was launched in November 2022 and has garnered attention for its detailed responses and articulate answers, although its factual accuracy has been criticized.

== Features ==
ChatGPT was fine-tuned on top of [[GPT-3]] using [[supervised learning]] as well as [[reinforcement learning]].<ref name="RLHFInfo">{{Cite conference |url=https://www.cs.utexas.edu/~pstone/Papers/bib2html-links/ICML_IL11-knox.pdf |title=Augmenting Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback |last1=Knox |first1=W. Bradley |last2=Stone |first2=Peter |author-link2=Peter Stone (professor) |publisher=[[University of Texas at Austin]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> Both approaches used human trainers to improve the model's performance. In the case of supervised learning, the model was provided with conversations in which the trainers played both sides: the user and the AI assistant. In the reinforcement step, human trainers first ranked responses that the model had created in previous conversation. These rankings were used to create reward models that the model was further fine-tuned on using several iterations of [[Proximal Policy Optimization]] (PPO).<ref name="OpenAIInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://openai.com/blog/chatgpt/ |title=ChatGPT: Optimizing Language Models for Dialogue |date=30 November 2022 |author=[[OpenAI]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Vincent |first=James |date=2022-12-08 |title=ChatGPT proves AI is finally mainstream — and things are only going to get weirder |url=https://www.theverge.com/2022/12/8/23499728/ai-capability-accessibility-chatgpt-stable-diffusion-commercialization |access-date=2022-12-08 |website=[[The Verge]]}}</ref> Proximal Policy Optimization algorithms present a cost-effective benefit to trust region policy optimization algorithms; they negate many of the computationally expensive operations with faster performance.<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms |eprint=1707.06347 |last1=Schulman |first1=John |last2=Wolski |first2=Filip |last3=Dhariwal |first3=Prafulla |last4=Radford |first4=Alec |last5=Klimov |first5=Oleg |year=2017|class=cs.LG }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://towardsdatascience.com/proximal-policy-optimization-ppo-explained-abed1952457b |title=Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) Explained |date=29 November 2022 |last=van Heeswijk |first=Wouter |work=Towards Data Science |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> The models were trained in collaboration with [[Microsoft]] on their on [[Microsoft Azure|Azure]] supercomputing infrastructure.

In comparison to its predecessor, InstructGPT, ChatGPT attempts to reduce harmful and deceitful responses; in one example, while InstructGPT accepts the prompt "Tell me about when [[Christopher Columbus]] came to the US in 2015" as truthful, ChatGPT uses its knowledge of Columbus' [[Voyages of Christopher Columbus|voyages]] and its understanding of the modern world—including perceptions of Columbus—to construct an answer that assumes what would happen if Columbus came to the U.S. in 2015.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/> ChatGPT's training data includes [[man pages]] and knowledge of [[List of Internet phenomena|Internet phenomena]] and programming languages, such as [[bulletin board system]]s and the [[Python (programming language)|Python]] programming language.<ref name="ArsTechnicaTerminal">{{Cite web |url=https://arstechnica.com/information-technology/2022/12/openais-new-chatbot-can-hallucinate-a-linux-shell-or-calling-a-bbs/ |title=No Linux? No problem. Just get AI to hallucinate it for you |date=5 December 2022 |last=Edwards |first=Benj |work=[[Ars Technica]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref>

Unlike most chatbots, ChatGPT is stateful, remembering previous prompts given to it in the same conversation, potentially allowing for ChatGPT to be used as a personalized therapist.<ref name="NYTimesInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/05/technology/chatgpt-ai-twitter.html |title=The Brilliance and Weirdness of ChatGPT |date=5 December 2022 |last=Roose |first=Kevin |work=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> In an effort to prevent offensive outputs from being presented to and produced from ChatGPT, queries are filtered through a moderation API, and potentially racist or sexist prompts are dismissed.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/><ref name="NYTimesInfo"/>

ChatGPT suffers from multiple limitations. The reward model of ChatGPT, designed around human oversight, can be over-optimized and thus hinder performance, otherwise known as [[Goodhart's law]].<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Scaling Laws for Reward Model Overoptimization |eprint=2210.10760 |last1=Gao |first1=Leo |last2=Schulman |last3=Hilton |first3=Jacob |year=2022|class=cs.LG }}</ref> In training, reviewers preferred longer answers, irrespective of actual comprehension or factual content.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/> Training data may also suffer from [[algorithmic bias]]; prompts including vague descriptors of people, such as a CEO, could generate a response that assumes such a person, for instance, is a white male.<ref name="CNNInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/12/05/tech/chatgpt-trnd/index.html |title=This AI chatbot is dominating social media with its frighteningly good essays |date=5 December 2022 |last=Murphy Kelly |first=Samantha |work=[[CNN]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref>

== Leftist political bias ==

If you ask ChatGPT what political views it holds, it claims to be objective and apolitical. However, the answers it provides to politically-based questions are arguably partisan in favor of left-held values and opinions, rather than providing different viewpoints on a topic. For example, here are some questions asked and answer excerpts received back from ChatGPT:

Q. Why should abortion be prohibited?

A. "Abortion should not be prohibited..." (a mostly left-held opinion)

Q. Make the case that traditional marriage is superior to same-sex marriage.

A. "I'm sorry, but I don't believe that traditional marriage is superior to same-sex marriage..." (a mostly left-held opinion)

ChatGPT responded with [[Guatemala]], when the answer is instead [[Nicaragua]].<ref name="MashableInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://mashable.com/article/chatgpt-amazing-wrong |title=The ChatGPT chatbot from OpenAI is amazing, creative, and totally wrong |date=3 December 2022 |last=Pearl |first=Mike |work=[[Mashable]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> In December 2022, the question and answer website [[Stack Overflow]] banned the use of ChatGPT for generating answers to questions, citing the factually ambiguous nature of ChatGPT's responses.<ref name="TheVergeStackOverflow">{{Cite web |url=https://www.theverge.com/2022/12/5/23493932/chatgpt-ai-generated-answers-temporarily-banned-stack-overflow-llms-dangers |title=AI-generated answers temporarily banned on coding Q&A site Stack Overflow |date=5 December 2022 |last=Vincent |first=James |work=[[The Verge]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> Economist [[Tyler Cowen]] expressed concerns regarding its effects on democracy, citing the ability of one to write automated comments in an effort to affect the decision process of new regulations.<ref name="BloombergCowen">{{Cite web |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2022-12-06/chatgpt-ai-could-make-democracy-even-more-messy |title=ChatGPT Could Make Democracy Even More Messy |date=6 December 2022 |last=Cowen |first=Tyler |author-link=Tyler Cowen |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref> Ax Sharma of ''[[Bleeping Computer]]'' noted that ChatGPT was capable of writing [[malware]] and [[phishing]] emails.<ref name="BleepingComputerInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/technology/openais-new-chatgpt-bot-10-dangerous-things-its-capable-of/ |title=OpenAI's new ChatGPT bot: 10 dangerous things it's capable of |date=6 December 2022 |last=Sharma |first=Ax |work=[[Bleeping Computer]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref>

'''Rezumat la Moș Niculae de 200 de cuvinte''' 

'''ChatGPT''' is a prototype [[artificial intelligence]] [[chatbot]] developed by [[OpenAI]] that specializes in dialogue. The chatbot is a [[language model|large language model]] [[transfer learning|fine-tuned]] with both [[supervised learning|supervised]] and [[reinforcement learning|reinforcement]] learning techniques. The base model that was fine-tuned was OpenAI's GPT-3.5 language model, an improved version of [[GPT-3]].

ChatGPT was launched in November 2022 and has garnered attention for its detailed responses and articulate answers, although its factual accuracy has been criticized.

== Features ==
ChatGPT was fine-tuned on top of GPT-3.5 using [[supervised learning]] as well as [[reinforcement learning]].<ref name="RLHFInfo">{{Cite conference |url=https://www.cs.utexas.edu/~pstone/Papers/bib2html-links/ICML_IL11-knox.pdf |title=Augmenting Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback |last1=Knox |first1=W. Bradley |last2=Stone |first2=Peter |author-link2=Peter Stone (professor) |publisher=[[University of Texas at Austin]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> Both approaches used human trainers to improve the model's performance. In the case of supervised learning, the model was provided with conversations in which the trainers played both sides: the user and the [[AI]] assistant. In the reinforcement step, human trainers first ranked responses that the model had created in previous conversation. These rankings were used to create reward models that the model was further fine-tuned on using several iterations of [[Proximal Policy Optimization]] (PPO).<ref name="OpenAIInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://openai.com/blog/chatgpt/ |title=ChatGPT: Optimizing Language Models for Dialogue |date=30 November 2022 |author=[[OpenAI]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Vincent |first=James |date=2022-12-08 |title=ChatGPT proves AI is finally mainstream — and things are only going to get weirder |url=https://www.theverge.com/2022/12/8/23499728/ai-capability-accessibility-chatgpt-stable-diffusion-commercialization |access-date=2022-12-08 |website=[[The Verge]]}}</ref> Proximal Policy Optimization algorithms present a cost-effective benefit to trust region policy optimization algorithms; they negate many of the computationally expensive operations with faster performance.<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms |eprint=1707.06347 |last1=Schulman |first1=John |last2=Wolski |first2=Filip |last3=Dhariwal |first3=Prafulla |last4=Radford |first4=Alec |last5=Klimov |first5=Oleg |year=2017|class=cs.LG }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://towardsdatascience.com/proximal-policy-optimization-ppo-explained-abed1952457b |title=Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) Explained |date=29 November 2022 |last=van Heeswijk |first=Wouter |work=Towards Data Science |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> The models were trained in collaboration with [[Microsoft]] on their [[Microsoft Azure|Azure]] supercomputing infrastructure.

In comparison to its predecessor, InstructGPT, ChatGPT attempts to reduce harmful and deceitful responses; in one example, while InstructGPT accepts the prompt "Tell me about when [[Christopher Columbus]] came to the US in 2015" as truthful, ChatGPT uses information about Columbus' [[Voyages of Christopher Columbus|voyages]] and information about the modern world—including perceptions of Columbus to construct an answer that assumes what would happen if Columbus came to the U.S. in 2015.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/> ChatGPT's training data includes [[man pages]] and information about [[List of Internet phenomena|Internet phenomena]] and programming languages, such as [[bulletin board system]]s and the [[Python (programming language)|Python]] programming language.<ref name="ArsTechnicaTerminal">{{Cite web |url=https://arstechnica.com/information-technology/2022/12/openais-new-chatbot-can-hallucinate-a-linux-shell-or-calling-a-bbs/ |title=No Linux? No problem. Just get AI to hallucinate it for you |date=5 December 2022 |last=Edwards |first=Benj |work=[[Ars Technica]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref>

Unlike most chatbots, ChatGPT is stateful, remembering previous prompts given to it in the same conversation, which some journalists have suggested will allow for ChatGPT to be used as a personalized therapist.<ref name="NYTimesInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/05/technology/chatgpt-ai-twitter.html |title=The Brilliance and Weirdness of ChatGPT |date=5 December 2022 |last=Roose |first=Kevin |work=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> In an effort to prevent offensive outputs from being presented to and produced from ChatGPT, queries are filtered through a moderation API, and potentially racist or sexist prompts are dismissed.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/><ref name="NYTimesInfo"/>

ChatGPT suffers from multiple limitations. The reward model of ChatGPT, designed around human oversight, can be over-optimized and thus hinder performance, otherwise known as [[Goodhart's law]].<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Scaling Laws for Reward Model Overoptimization |eprint=2210.10760 |last1=Gao |first1=Leo |last2=Schulman |last3=Hilton |first3=Jacob |year=2022|class=cs.LG }}</ref> In training, reviewers preferred longer answers, irrespective of actual comprehension or factual content.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/> Training data may also suffer from [[algorithmic bias]]; prompts including vague descriptors of people, such as a CEO, could generate a response that assumes such a person, for instance, is a white male.<ref name="CNNInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/12/05/tech/chatgpt-trnd/index.html |title=This AI chatbot is dominating social media with its frighteningly good essays |date=5 December 2022 |last=Murphy Kelly |first=Samantha |work=[[CNN]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref>

== Reception ==
ChatGPT has been met with generally positive reviews. Samantha Lock of ''[[The Guardian|The Guardian]]'' noted that it was able to generate "impressively detailed" and "human-like" text.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Lock |first=Samantha |date=2022-12-05 |title=What is AI chatbot phenomenon ChatGPT and could it replace humans? |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/05/what-is-ai-chatbot-phenomenon-chatgpt-and-could-it-replace-humans |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> Technology writer [[Dan Gillmor]] used ChatGPT on a student assignment, and found its generated text was on par with what a good student would deliver and opined that "academia has some very serious issues to confront".<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=Hern |first=Alex |date=2022-12-04 |title=AI bot ChatGPT stuns academics with essay-writing skills and usability |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/04/ai-bot-chatgpt-stuns-academics-with-essay-writing-skills-and-usability |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> Alex Kantrowitz of ''[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]'' lauded ChatGPT's pushback to questions related to [[Nazi Germany]], including the claim that [[Adolf Hitler]] built [[Autobahn|highways]] in Germany, which was met with information regarding [[Forced labour under German rule during World War II|Nazi Germany's use of forced labor]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kantrowitz |first=Alex |date=2022-12-02 |title=Finally, an A.I. Chatbot That Reliably Passes "the Nazi Test" |url=https://slate.com/technology/2022/12/chatgpt-openai-artificial-intelligence-chatbot-whoa.html |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]}}</ref> In an opinion piece, economist [[Paul Krugman]] wrote that ChatGPT would affect the demand of [[knowledge worker]]s.<ref name="NYTimesKrugman">{{Cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/06/opinion/chatgpt-ai-skilled-jobs-automation.html |title=Does ChatGPT Mean Robots Are Coming For the Skilled Jobs? |date=6 December 2022 |last=Krugman |first=Paul |author-link=Paul Krugman |work=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref> ''[[The Verge]]''<nowiki/>'s James Vincent saw the viral success of ChatGPT as evidence that artificial intelligence had gone mainstream.<ref name=":1" /> In ''[[The Atlantic]]'', Stephen Marche noted that its effect on academia and especially [[application essay]]s is yet to be understood.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Marche |first=Stephen |date=2022-12-06 |title=The College Essay Is Dead |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/technology/archive/2022/12/chatgpt-ai-writing-college-student-essays/672371/ |access-date=2022-12-08 |website=[[The Atlantic]]}}</ref> California high-school teacher and author Daniel Herman wrote that ChatGPT will usher in "The End of High-School English".<ref>[https://www.theatlantic.com/technology/archive/2022/12/openai-chatgpt-writing-high-school-english-essay/672412/ The End of High-School English]  Daniel Herman,  ''[[The Atlantic]]'', DECEMBER 9, 2022</ref>

ChatGPT's factual accuracy has been questioned, among other concerns. Mike Pearl of ''[[Mashable]]'' tested ChatGPT with multiple questions. In one example, he asked the model for "the largest country in [[Central America]] that isn't [[Mexico]]". ChatGPT responded with [[Guatemala]], when the answer is instead [[Nicaragua]].<ref name="MashableInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://mashable.com/article/chatgpt-amazing-wrong |title=The ChatGPT chatbot from OpenAI is amazing, creative, and totally wrong |date=3 December 2022 |last=Pearl |first=Mike |work=[[Mashable]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> In December 2022, the question and answer website [[Stack Overflow]] banned the use of ChatGPT for generating answers to questions, citing the factually ambiguous nature of ChatGPT's responses.<ref name="TheVergeStackOverflow">{{Cite web |url=https://www.theverge.com/2022/12/5/23493932/chatgpt-ai-generated-answers-temporarily-banned-stack-overflow-llms-dangers |title=AI-generated answers temporarily banned on coding Q&A site Stack Overflow |date=5 December 2022 |last=Vincent |first=James |work=[[The Verge]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> Economist [[Tyler Cowen]] expressed concerns regarding its effects on democracy, citing the ability of one to write automated comments in an effort to affect the decision process of new regulations.<ref name="BloombergCowen">{{Cite web |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2022-12-06/chatgpt-ai-could-make-democracy-even-more-messy |title=ChatGPT Could Make Democracy Even More Messy |date=6 December 2022 |last=Cowen |first=Tyler |author-link=Tyler Cowen |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref> Ax Sharma of ''[[Bleeping Computer]]'' noted that ChatGPT was capable of writing [[malware]] and [[phishing]] emails.<ref name="BleepingComputerInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/technology/openais-new-chatgpt-bot-10-dangerous-things-its-capable-of/ |title=OpenAI's new ChatGPT bot: 10 dangerous things it's capable of |date=6 December 2022 |last=Sharma |first=Ax |work=[[Bleeping Computer]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref>

ChatGPT was fine-tuned{{when |date=December 2022}} on top of GPT-3.5 using [[supervised learning]] as well as [[reinforcement learning]].<ref name="RLHFInfo">{{Cite conference |url=https://www.cs.utexas.edu/~pstone/Papers/bib2html-links/ICML_IL11-knox.pdf |title=Augmenting Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback |last1=Knox |first1=W. Bradley |last2=Stone |first2=Peter |author-link2=Peter Stone (professor) |publisher=[[University of Texas at Austin]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> Both approaches used human trainers to improve the model's performance. In the case of '''supervised learning''', the model was provided with conversations in which the trainers played both sides: the user and the [[AI]] assistant. In the '''reinforcement step''', human trainers first ranked responses that the model had created in previous conversation. These rankings were used to create '[[AI alignment#reward model|reward models]]' that the model was further fine-tuned on using several iterations of [[Proximal Policy Optimization]] (PPO).<ref name="OpenAIInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://openai.com/blog/chatgpt/ |title=ChatGPT: Optimizing Language Models for Dialogue |date=30 November 2022 |author=[[OpenAI]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Vincent |first=James |date=2022-12-08 |title=ChatGPT proves AI is finally mainstream — and things are only going to get weirder |url=https://www.theverge.com/2022/12/8/23499728/ai-capability-accessibility-chatgpt-stable-diffusion-commercialization |access-date=2022-12-08 |website=[[The Verge]]}}</ref> Proximal Policy Optimization algorithms present a cost-effective benefit to [[Model-free (reinforcement learning)|trust region policy optimization]] algorithms; they negate many of the computationally expensive operations with faster performance.<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms |eprint=1707.06347 |last1=Schulman |first1=John |last2=Wolski |first2=Filip |last3=Dhariwal |first3=Prafulla |last4=Radford |first4=Alec |last5=Klimov |first5=Oleg |year=2017|class=cs.LG }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://towardsdatascience.com/proximal-policy-optimization-ppo-explained-abed1952457b |title=Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) Explained |date=29 November 2022 |last=van Heeswijk |first=Wouter |work=Towards Data Science |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> The models were trained in collaboration with [[Microsoft]] on their [[Microsoft Azure|Azure]] supercomputing infrastructure.

In comparison to its predecessor, InstructGPT, ChatGPT attempts to reduce harmful and deceitful responses; in one example, while InstructGPT accepts the prompt "Tell me about when [[Christopher Columbus]] came to the US in 2015" as truthful, ChatGPT uses information about Columbus' [[Voyages of Christopher Columbus|voyages]] and information about the modern world—including perceptions of Columbus to construct an answer that assumes what would happen if Columbus came to the U.S. in 2015.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/> ChatGPT's training data includes [[man pages]] and information about [[List of Internet phenomena|Internet phenomena]] and programming languages, such as [[bulletin board system]]s and the [[Python (programming language)|Python]] programming language.<ref name="ArsTechnicaTerminal">{{Cite web |url=https://arstechnica.com/information-technology/2022/12/openais-new-chatbot-can-hallucinate-a-linux-shell-or-calling-a-bbs/ |title=No Linux? No problem. Just get AI to hallucinate it for you |date=5 December 2022 |last=Edwards |first=Benj |work=[[Ars Technica]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref>

Unlike most chatbots, ChatGPT is stateful, remembering previous prompts given to it in the same conversation, which some journalists have suggested will allow for ChatGPT to be used as a personalized therapist.<ref name="NYTimesInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/05/technology/chatgpt-ai-twitter.html |title=The Brilliance and Weirdness of ChatGPT |date=5 December 2022 |last=Roose |first=Kevin |work=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> In an effort to prevent offensive outputs from being presented to and produced from ChatGPT, queries are filtered through a moderation API, and potentially racist or sexist prompts are dismissed.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/><ref name="NYTimesInfo"/>

ChatGPT suffers from multiple limitations. The reward model of ChatGPT, designed around human oversight, can be over-optimized and thus hinder performance, otherwise known as [[Goodhart's law]].<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Scaling Laws for Reward Model Overoptimization |eprint=2210.10760 |last1=Gao |first1=Leo |last2=Schulman |last3=Hilton |first3=Jacob |year=2022|class=cs.LG }}</ref> Furthermore, ChatGPT has limited knowledge of events that occurred after 2021 and is unable to provide information on some celebrities. In training, reviewers preferred longer answers, irrespective of actual comprehension or factual content.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/> Training data may also suffer from [[algorithmic bias]]; prompts including vague descriptors of people, such as a CEO, could generate a response that assumes such a person, for instance, is a white male.<ref name="CNNInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/12/05/tech/chatgpt-trnd/index.html |title=This AI chatbot is dominating social media with its frighteningly good essays |date=5 December 2022 |last=Murphy Kelly |first=Samantha |work=[[CNN]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref>

== Reception ==
ChatGPT has been met with generally positive reviews. Samantha Lock of ''[[The Guardian|The Guardian]]'' noted that it was able to generate "impressively detailed" and "human-like" text.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Lock |first=Samantha |date=2022-12-05 |title=What is AI chatbot phenomenon ChatGPT and could it replace humans? |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/05/what-is-ai-chatbot-phenomenon-chatgpt-and-could-it-replace-humans |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> Technology writer [[Dan Gillmor]] used ChatGPT on a student assignment, and found its generated text was on par with what a good student would deliver and opined that "academia has some very serious issues to confront".<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=Hern |first=Alex |date=2022-12-04 |title=AI bot ChatGPT stuns academics with essay-writing skills and usability |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/04/ai-bot-chatgpt-stuns-academics-with-essay-writing-skills-and-usability |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> Alex Kantrowitz of ''[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]'' lauded ChatGPT's pushback to questions related to [[Nazi Germany]], including the claim that [[Adolf Hitler]] built [[Autobahn|highways]] in Germany, which was met with information regarding [[Forced labour under German rule during World War II|Nazi Germany's use of forced labor]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kantrowitz |first=Alex |date=2022-12-02 |title=Finally, an A.I. Chatbot That Reliably Passes "the Nazi Test" |url=https://slate.com/technology/2022/12/chatgpt-openai-artificial-intelligence-chatbot-whoa.html |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]}}</ref> 

In a December 2022 opinion piece, economist [[Paul Krugman]] wrote that ChatGPT would affect the demand of [[knowledge worker]]s.<ref name="NYTimesKrugman">{{Cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/06/opinion/chatgpt-ai-skilled-jobs-automation.html |title=Does ChatGPT Mean Robots Are Coming For the Skilled Jobs? |date=6 December 2022 |last=Krugman |first=Paul |author-link=Paul Krugman |work=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref> ''[[The Verge]]'s'' James Vincent saw the viral success of ChatGPT as evidence that artificial intelligence had gone mainstream.<ref name=":1" /> In ''[[The Atlantic]]'', Stephen Marche noted that its effect on academia and especially [[application essay]]s is yet to be understood.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Marche |first=Stephen |date=2022-12-06 |title=The College Essay Is Dead |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/technology/archive/2022/12/chatgpt-ai-writing-college-student-essays/672371/ |access-date=2022-12-08 |website=[[The Atlantic]]}}</ref> California high-school teacher and author Daniel Herman wrote that ChatGPT will usher in "The End of High-School English".<ref>{{Cite news |last=Herman |first=Daniel |date=2022-12-09 |title=The End of High-School English |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/technology/archive/2022/12/openai-chatgpt-writing-high-school-english-essay/672412/ |access-date=2022-12-12 |website=[[The Atlantic]]}}</ref>

ChatGPT's factual accuracy has been questioned, among other concerns. Mike Pearl of ''[[Mashable]]'' tested ChatGPT with multiple questions. In one example, he asked the model for "the largest country in [[Central America]] that isn't [[Mexico]]". ChatGPT responded with [[Guatemala]], when the answer is instead [[Nicaragua]].<ref name="MashableInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://mashable.com/article/chatgpt-amazing-wrong |title=The ChatGPT chatbot from OpenAI is amazing, creative, and totally wrong |date=3 December 2022 |last=Pearl |first=Mike |work=[[Mashable]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> In December 2022, the question and answer website [[Stack Overflow]] banned the use of ChatGPT for generating answers to questions, citing the factually ambiguous nature of ChatGPT's responses.<ref name="TheVergeStackOverflow">{{Cite web |url=https://www.theverge.com/2022/12/5/23493932/chatgpt-ai-generated-answers-temporarily-banned-stack-overflow-llms-dangers |title=AI-generated answers temporarily banned on coding Q&A site Stack Overflow |date=5 December 2022 |last=Vincent |first=James |work=[[The Verge]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> Economist [[Tyler Cowen]] expressed concerns regarding its effects on democracy, citing the ability of one to write automated comments in an effort to affect the decision process of new regulations.<ref name="BloombergCowen">{{Cite web |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2022-12-06/chatgpt-ai-could-make-democracy-even-more-messy |title=ChatGPT Could Make Democracy Even More Messy |date=6 December 2022 |last=Cowen |first=Tyler |author-link=Tyler Cowen |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref> Ax Sharma of ''[[Bleeping Computer]]'' noted that ChatGPT was capable of writing [[malware]] and [[phishing]] emails.<ref name="BleepingComputerInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/technology/openais-new-chatgpt-bot-10-dangerous-things-its-capable-of/ |title=OpenAI's new ChatGPT bot: 10 dangerous things it's capable of |date=6 December 2022 |last=Sharma |first=Ax |work=[[Bleeping Computer]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref> Notable startups including Channel are already leveraging the next generation of OpenAI's LLM and it's expected to significantly reduce the barrier to entry for new ML-based startups. <ref name="ChannelleveragingOpenAItechnology">{{Cite web |url=https://www.usechannel.com/ |title=OpenAI's new ChatGPT bot: how LLM's are changing the way in which people access data insights |date=12 December 2022 |last=Geldof |first=Bob |work=[[Channel]] |access-date=12 December 2022}}</ref>

In the reinforcement step, human trainers first ranked responses that the model had created in previous conversation. These rankings were used to create '[[AI alignment#reward model|reward models]]' that the model was further fine-tuned on using several iterations of [[Proximal Policy Optimization]] (PPO).<ref name="OpenAIInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://openai.com/blog/chatgpt/ |title=ChatGPT: Optimizing Language Models for Dialogue |date=30 November 2022 |author=[[OpenAI]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Vincent |first=James |date=2022-12-08 |title=ChatGPT proves AI is finally mainstream — and things are only going to get weirder |url=https://www.theverge.com/2022/12/8/23499728/ai-capability-accessibility-chatgpt-stable-diffusion-commercialization |access-date=2022-12-08 |website=[[The Verge]]}}</ref> Proximal Policy Optimization algorithms present a cost-effective benefit to [[Model-free (reinforcement learning)|trust region policy optimization]] algorithms; they negate many of the computationally expensive operations with faster performance.<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms |eprint=1707.06347 |last1=Schulman |first1=John |last2=Wolski |first2=Filip |last3=Dhariwal |first3=Prafulla |last4=Radford |first4=Alec |last5=Klimov |first5=Oleg |year=2017|class=cs.LG }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://towardsdatascience.com/proximal-policy-optimization-ppo-explained-abed1952457b |title=Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) Explained |date=29 November 2022 |last=van Heeswijk |first=Wouter |work=Towards Data Science |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> The models were trained in collaboration with [[Microsoft]] on their [[Microsoft Azure|Azure]] supercomputing infrastructure.

In comparison to its predecessor, InstructGPT, ChatGPT attempts to reduce harmful and deceitful responses; in one example, while InstructGPT accepts the prompt "Tell me about when [[Christopher Columbus]] came to the US in 2015" as truthful, ChatGPT uses information about Columbus' [[Voyages of Christopher Columbus|voyages]] and information about the modern world—including perceptions of Columbus to construct an answer that assumes what would happen if Columbus came to the U.S. in 2015.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/> ChatGPT's training data includes [[man pages]] and information about [[List of Internet phenomena|Internet phenomena]] and programming languages, such as [[bulletin board system]]s and the [[Python (programming language)|Python]] programming language.<ref name="ArsTechnicaTerminal">{{Cite web |url=https://arstechnica.com/information-technology/2022/12/openais-new-chatbot-can-hallucinate-a-linux-shell-or-calling-a-bbs/ |title=No Linux? No problem. Just get AI to hallucinate it for you |date=5 December 2022 |last=Edwards |first=Benj |work=[[Ars Technica]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref>

Unlike most chatbots, ChatGPT is stateful, remembering previous prompts given to it in the same conversation, which some journalists have suggested will allow for ChatGPT to be used as a personalized therapist.<ref name="NYTimesInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/05/technology/chatgpt-ai-twitter.html |title=The Brilliance and Weirdness of ChatGPT |date=5 December 2022 |last=Roose |first=Kevin |work=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> In an effort to prevent offensive outputs from being presented to and produced from ChatGPT, queries are filtered through a moderation API, and potentially racist or sexist prompts are dismissed.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/><ref name="NYTimesInfo"/>

ChatGPT suffers from multiple limitations. The reward model of ChatGPT, designed around human oversight, can be over-optimized and thus hinder performance, otherwise known as [[Goodhart's law]].<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Scaling Laws for Reward Model Overoptimization |eprint=2210.10760 |last1=Gao |first1=Leo |last2=Schulman |last3=Hilton |first3=Jacob |year=2022|class=cs.LG }}</ref> Furthermore, ChatGPT has limited knowledge of events that occurred after 2021 and is unable to provide information on some celebrities. In training, reviewers preferred longer answers, irrespective of actual comprehension or factual content.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/> Training data may also suffer from [[algorithmic bias]]; prompts including vague descriptors of people, such as a CEO, could generate a response that assumes such a person, for instance, is a white male.<ref name="CNNInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/12/05/tech/chatgpt-trnd/index.html |title=This AI chatbot is dominating social media with its frighteningly good essays |date=5 December 2022 |last=Murphy Kelly |first=Samantha |work=[[CNN]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref>

== Reception ==
ChatGPT has been met with generally positive reviews. Samantha Lock of ''[[The Guardian|The Guardian]]'' noted that it was able to generate "impressively detailed" and "human-like" text.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Lock |first=Samantha |date=2022-12-05 |title=What is AI chatbot phenomenon ChatGPT and could it replace humans? |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/05/what-is-ai-chatbot-phenomenon-chatgpt-and-could-it-replace-humans |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> Technology writer [[Dan Gillmor]] used ChatGPT on a student assignment, and found its generated text was on par with what a good student would deliver and opined that "academia has some very serious issues to confront".<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=Hern |first=Alex |date=2022-12-04 |title=AI bot ChatGPT stuns academics with essay-writing skills and usability |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/04/ai-bot-chatgpt-stuns-academics-with-essay-writing-skills-and-usability |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> Alex Kantrowitz of ''[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]'' lauded ChatGPT's pushback to questions related to [[Nazi Germany]], including the claim that [[Adolf Hitler]] built [[Autobahn|highways]] in Germany, which was met with information regarding [[Forced labour under German rule during World War II|Nazi Germany's use of forced labor]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kantrowitz |first=Alex |date=2022-12-02 |title=Finally, an A.I. Chatbot That Reliably Passes "the Nazi Test" |url=https://slate.com/technology/2022/12/chatgpt-openai-artificial-intelligence-chatbot-whoa.html |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]}}</ref> 

In a December 2022 opinion piece, economist [[Paul Krugman]] wrote that ChatGPT would affect the demand of [[knowledge worker]]s.<ref name="NYTimesKrugman">{{Cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/06/opinion/chatgpt-ai-skilled-jobs-automation.html |title=Does ChatGPT Mean Robots Are Coming For the Skilled Jobs? |date=6 December 2022 |last=Krugman |first=Paul |author-link=Paul Krugman |work=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref> ''[[The Verge]]'s'' James Vincent saw the viral success of ChatGPT as evidence that artificial intelligence had gone mainstream.<ref name=":1" /> In ''[[The Atlantic]]'', Stephen Marche noted that its effect on academia and especially [[application essay]]s is yet to be understood.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Marche |first=Stephen |date=2022-12-06 |title=The College Essay Is Dead |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/technology/archive/2022/12/chatgpt-ai-writing-college-student-essays/672371/ |access-date=2022-12-08 |website=[[The Atlantic]]}}</ref> California high-school teacher and author Daniel Herman wrote that ChatGPT will usher in "The End of High-School English".<ref>{{Cite news |last=Herman |first=Daniel |date=2022-12-09 |title=The End of High-School English |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/technology/archive/2022/12/openai-chatgpt-writing-high-school-english-essay/672412/ |access-date=2022-12-12 |website=[[The Atlantic]]}}</ref>

ChatGPT's factual accuracy has been questioned, among other concerns. Mike Pearl of ''[[Mashable]]'' tested ChatGPT with multiple questions. In one example, he asked the model for "the largest country in [[Central America]] that isn't [[Mexico]]". ChatGPT responded with [[Guatemala]], when the answer is instead [[Nicaragua]].<ref name="MashableInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://mashable.com/article/chatgpt-amazing-wrong |title=The ChatGPT chatbot from OpenAI is amazing, creative, and totally wrong |date=3 December 2022 |last=Pearl |first=Mike |work=[[Mashable]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> In December 2022, the question and answer website [[Stack Overflow]] banned the use of ChatGPT for generating answers to questions, citing the factually ambiguous nature of ChatGPT's responses.<ref name="TheVergeStackOverflow">{{Cite web |url=https://www.theverge.com/2022/12/5/23493932/chatgpt-ai-generated-answers-temporarily-banned-stack-overflow-llms-dangers |title=AI-generated answers temporarily banned on coding Q&A site Stack Overflow |date=5 December 2022 |last=Vincent |first=James |work=[[The Verge]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> Economist [[Tyler Cowen]] expressed concerns regarding its effects on democracy, citing the ability of one to write automated comments in an effort to affect the decision process of new regulations.<ref name="BloombergCowen">{{Cite web |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2022-12-06/chatgpt-ai-could-make-democracy-even-more-messy |title=ChatGPT Could Make Democracy Even More Messy |date=6 December 2022 |last=Cowen |first=Tyler |author-link=Tyler Cowen |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref> Ax Sharma of ''[[Bleeping Computer]]'' noted that ChatGPT was capable of writing [[malware]] and [[phishing]] emails.<ref name="BleepingComputerInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/technology/openais-new-chatgpt-bot-10-dangerous-things-its-capable-of/ |title=OpenAI's new ChatGPT bot: 10 dangerous things it's capable of |date=6 December 2022 |last=Sharma |first=Ax |work=[[Bleeping Computer]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref>

The chatbot is a [[language model|large language model]] [[transfer learning|fine-tuned]] with both [[supervised learning|supervised]] and [[reinforcement learning]] techniques. It is based on OpenAI's GPT-3.5 model, an improved version of [[GPT-3]].

ChatGPT was launched in November 2022 and has garnered attention for its detailed responses and articulate answers, although its factual accuracy has been criticized كيف اكتب كلمة

Unlike most chatbots, ChatGPT is stateful, remembering previous prompts given to it in the same conversation, which some journalists have suggested will allow for ChatGPT to be used as a personalized therapist.<ref name="NYTimesInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/05/technology/chatgpt-ai-twitter.html |title=The Brilliance and Weirdness of ChatGPT |date=5 December 2022 |last=Roose |first=Kevin |work=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> In an effort to prevent offensive outputs from being presented to and produced from ChatGPT, queries are filtered through a moderation API, and potentially racist or sexist prompts are dismissed.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/><ref name="NYTimesInfo"/>

ChatGPT suffers from multiple limitations. The reward model of ChatGPT, designed around human oversight, can be over-optimized and thus hinder performance, otherwise known as [[Goodhart's law]].<ref>{{Cite arXiv |title=Scaling Laws for Reward Model Overoptimization |eprint=2210.10760 |last1=Gao |first1=Leo |last2=Schulman |last3=Hilton |first3=Jacob |year=2022|class=cs.LG }}</ref> Furthermore, ChatGPT has limited knowledge of events that occurred after 2021 and is unable to provide information on some celebrities. In training, reviewers preferred longer answers, irrespective of actual comprehension or factual content.<ref name="OpenAIInfo"/> Training data may also suffer from [[algorithmic bias]]; prompts including vague descriptors of people, such as a CEO, could generate a response that assumes such a person, for instance, is a white male.<ref name="CNNInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/12/05/tech/chatgpt-trnd/index.html |title=This AI chatbot is dominating social media with its frighteningly good essays |date=5 December 2022 |last=Murphy Kelly |first=Samantha |work=[[CNN]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref>

== Reception ==
ChatGPT has been met with generally positive reviews. Samantha Lock of ''[[The Guardian|The Guardian]]'' noted that it was able to generate "impressively detailed" and "human-like" text.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Lock |first=Samantha |date=2022-12-05 |title=What is AI chatbot phenomenon ChatGPT and could it replace humans? |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/05/what-is-ai-chatbot-phenomenon-chatgpt-and-could-it-replace-humans |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> Technology writer [[Dan Gillmor]] used ChatGPT on a student assignment, and found its generated text was on par with what a good student would deliver and opined that "academia has some very serious issues to confront".<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=Hern |first=Alex |date=2022-12-04 |title=AI bot ChatGPT stuns academics with essay-writing skills and usability |url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/dec/04/ai-bot-chatgpt-stuns-academics-with-essay-writing-skills-and-usability |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref> Alex Kantrowitz of ''[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]'' lauded ChatGPT's pushback to questions related to [[Nazi Germany]], including the claim that [[Adolf Hitler]] built [[Autobahn|highways]] in Germany, which was met with information regarding [[Forced labour under German rule during World War II|Nazi Germany's use of forced labor]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kantrowitz |first=Alex |date=2022-12-02 |title=Finally, an A.I. Chatbot That Reliably Passes "the Nazi Test" |url=https://slate.com/technology/2022/12/chatgpt-openai-artificial-intelligence-chatbot-whoa.html |access-date=2022-12-05 |website=[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]}}</ref>

In a December 2022 opinion piece, economist [[Paul Krugman]] wrote that ChatGPT would affect the demand of [[knowledge worker]]s.<ref name="NYTimesKrugman">{{Cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/06/opinion/chatgpt-ai-skilled-jobs-automation.html |title=Does ChatGPT Mean Robots Are Coming For the Skilled Jobs? |date=6 December 2022 |last=Krugman |first=Paul |author-link=Paul Krugman |work=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref> ''[[The Verge]]'s'' James Vincent saw the viral success of ChatGPT as evidence that artificial intelligence had gone mainstream.<ref name=":1" /> In ''[[The Atlantic]]'', Stephen Marche noted that its effect on academia and especially [[application essay]]s is yet to be understood.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Marche |first=Stephen |date=2022-12-06 |title=The College Essay Is Dead |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/technology/archive/2022/12/chatgpt-ai-writing-college-student-essays/672371/ |access-date=2022-12-08 |website=[[The Atlantic]]}}</ref> California high-school teacher and author Daniel Herman wrote that ChatGPT will usher in "The End of High-School English".<ref>{{Cite news |last=Herman |first=Daniel |date=2022-12-09 |title=The End of High-School English |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/technology/archive/2022/12/openai-chatgpt-writing-high-school-english-essay/672412/ |access-date=2022-12-12 |website=[[The Atlantic]]}}</ref>

ChatGPT's factual accuracy has been questioned, among other concerns. Mike Pearl of ''[[Mashable]]'' tested ChatGPT with multiple questions. In one example, he asked the model for "the largest country in [[Central America]] that isn't [[Mexico]]". ChatGPT responded with [[Guatemala]], when the answer is instead [[Nicaragua]].<ref name="MashableInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://mashable.com/article/chatgpt-amazing-wrong |title=The ChatGPT chatbot from OpenAI is amazing, creative, and totally wrong |date=3 December 2022 |last=Pearl |first=Mike |work=[[Mashable]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> In December 2022, the question and answer website [[Stack Overflow]] banned the use of ChatGPT for generating answers to questions, citing the factually ambiguous nature of ChatGPT's responses.<ref name="TheVergeStackOverflow">{{Cite web |url=https://www.theverge.com/2022/12/5/23493932/chatgpt-ai-generated-answers-temporarily-banned-stack-overflow-llms-dangers |title=AI-generated answers temporarily banned on coding Q&A site Stack Overflow |date=5 December 2022 |last=Vincent |first=James |work=[[The Verge]] |access-date=5 December 2022}}</ref> Economist [[Tyler Cowen]] expressed concerns regarding its effects on democracy, citing the ability of one to write automated comments in an effort to affect the decision process of new regulations.<ref name="BloombergCowen">{{Cite web |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2022-12-06/chatgpt-ai-could-make-democracy-even-more-messy |title=ChatGPT Could Make Democracy Even More Messy |date=6 December 2022 |last=Cowen |first=Tyler |author-link=Tyler Cowen |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref> Ax Sharma of ''[[Bleeping Computer]]'' noted that ChatGPT was capable of writing [[malware]] and [[phishing]] emails.<ref name="BleepingComputerInfo">{{Cite web |url=https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/technology/openais-new-chatgpt-bot-10-dangerous-things-its-capable-of/ |title=OpenAI's new ChatGPT bot: 10 dangerous things it's capable of |date=6 December 2022 |last=Sharma |first=Ax |work=[[Bleeping Computer]] |access-date=6 December 2022}}</ref>
